radicals.meaningMnemonic: Imagine this in your mouth. It's one tooth that sticks out more than any other. It's your <radical>fang</radical>. Can you see this sticking out of a wolf mouth instead? Try that if you can't picture yourself with a fang this cool.

radicals.meaningMnemonic: He's a creeper and he's Four-T (forty). If someone like this creeps on you, they're just a <radical>bully</radical>. When you get to forty you aren't supposed to creep on people anymore. You bully. You jerk.

radicals.meaningMnemonic: See how there’s a lid on top of the treasure chest? That’s because the whole thing is a <radical>rocket</radical> which contains all the necessary items (treasures) to live in outer space. The bottom part of the radical shows you that this is a top secret project, though, so keep this private in your own head.

radicals.meaningMnemonic: There's a door over the bookcase. That makes this a <radical>library</radical>!

radicals.meaningMnemonic:
This radical means <radical>stomach</radical>, since it kind of looks like one. See the big stomach sitting on top of some intestines that snake downwards? Just keep in mind this radical is specifically for the stomach, and the stomach only — not all the other stuff attached to it.

You could also remember it with a mnemonic: To eat a rice paddy the size of the moon, you'd need a pretty impressive stomach. Every doctor and scientist in the world would probably be jostling to get a look at your incredible stomach and see how you managed to fit a moon-sized rice paddy into it.

You could also remember it with a mnemonic: To eat a rice paddy the size of the moon, you'd need a pretty impressive stomach. Every doctor and scientist in the world would probably be jostling to get a look at your incredible stomach and see how you managed to fit a moon-sized rice paddy into it.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> with <radical>direction</radical> will have things <kanji>obstruct</kanji> her path. It's a sad fact that it's still a man's world out there!
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this woman with direction, knocking down obstruction after obstruction on her path to success.
kanji.readingMnemonic: This woman is truly magnificent. Some compare her to a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball, because of how she knocks down the obstructions in her path like bowling pins.
kanji.readingHint: Now imagine that she actually is a bowling ball. Watch her roll down the bowling lane and smash all those stupid pins!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>stomach</radical> and the kanji <kanji>stomach</kanji> are exactly the same!
kanji.meaningHint: The kanji that you already know are the easiest ones to stomach.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Inside your <kanji>stomach</kanji>, you secretly keep an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) to help you digest things. As one of nature's top predators, an eagle can consume just about any food you throw at it, so it's really handy to have one living in your stomach.
kanji.readingHint: Whenever you put food into your stomach, your eagle tears it to shreds with its beak and pre-processes it for you. Sometimes it accidentally pecks you, though, which is a little painful.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>rocket</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> has several points which will <kanji>detach</kanji> during takeoff.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the rocket turkey taking off. Just like a regular rocket, as it goes up, used pieces of the turkey rocket detach and fall back to the ground.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When the rocket turkey parts <kanji>detach</kanji>, they fall to the ground onto a bed of <reading>ree</reading>ds (り).
kanji.readingHint: The reeds catch on fire - probably best not to fire off turkey rockets so close to these fields of reeds. Imagine the launch site completely surrounded by reeds.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>bully</radical> take an <radical>insect</radical> and <kanji>dissolve</kanji> it in some acid. Bullies are messed up, mean people. They do awful things that normal kids and people wouldn't do... like dissolve a living creature.
kanji.meaningHint: Actually... try to forget this one. Don't think about a bully dissolving things in acid. Think happy thoughts. Like... the bully getting dissolved instead and all the little insects coming back to life and being happy.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The only bullies heartless enough to <kanji>dissolve</kanji> insects like this are <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). Do you really think an adult would melt a harmless critter? No. Only inexperienced youths could be so cruel.
kanji.readingHint: When you first read this and you pictured the bully dissolving the insect, you pictured them as a youth, right? See? It makes complete sense.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Bring <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>library</radical> to <kanji>knit</kanji>. Libraries are quiet and generally pretty empty nowadays, so it's the perfect place to relax and do some knitting. Of course, if you find thread lying around in a library you know what to do now.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself sitting on a rocking chair in the library knitting away. It's quiet and peaceful and the light is warming you just right. Ahh, bliss!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You need to come to the library to <kanji>knit</kanji> because if you try to do it at home all your <reading>hen</reading>s (へん) will try to eat the thread. Hens love eating things that look like worms, like hay, string, and thread. So it's important to get far away from them before you start to knit.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the granny or grampy you are (and must be to have hens and want to knit in peace in a library). You're tired of these hens eating your thread while you try to knit sweaters for all your grandbabies so you come to the library.

rawData.meaning_hint: Picture yourself sitting on a rocking chair in the library knitting away. It's quiet and peaceful and the light is warming you just right. Ahh, bliss!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> growing out of a <radical>rice paddy</radical> into the shape of a <radical>cross</radical> are really <kanji>showy</kanji> <kanji>flower</kanji>s.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture these showy flowers growing straight up and out of the middle of the rice paddy, into the shape of a giant cross.
kanji.readingMnemonic: These <kanji>flower</kanji>s are too <kanji>showy</kanji>. You hate them. You hate these showy flowers so much you run them over with your <reading>ca</reading>r (か)!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine how the car feels as you ram into the showy flowers. The petals explode and rain everywhere. You turn on your windshield wipers to push them away.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>root</radical> where your <radical>fang</radical> <kanji>previously</kanji> was. Your fang isn't there anymore, you ripped it out.
kanji.meaningHint: Look at the root where your fang previously was. Previously, when you were young, you had a lot of nice fangs but they're all rotting as you get older and you pull them out.
kanji.readingMnemonic: To remember the beautiful fangs you <kanji>previously</kanji> had, you keep all of the ones you pull out in a box you lock with a <reading>key</reading> (き).
kanji.readingHint: Previously you didn't have a key, but now you do to keep your old fangs safe.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>line up</radical> your body with the <radical>sun</radical> and everything looks <kanji>normal</kanji>.  The sun is lined up behind you, and you’re looking at all the things the sun has lit up. Everything looks normal.
kanji.meaningHint: Gaze out in a line from the sun as you observe the world. Everything is very normal looking and comforting.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Soon you see something that doesn’t look so <kanji>normal</kanji>. It’s a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ), a jester. Dressed all in fool’s garb, this fool is dancing up towards you with a ridiculously unsettling grin on his face.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine this creepy looking fool as he approaches. He’s kind of freaking you out. Feel very uncomfortable as you continue looking at this fool.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You put a <radical>lid</radical> on a dish you're cooking, made up of the <radical>mouth</radical>s and <radical>forehead</radical>s of a <radical>pig</radical>. This is truly an <kanji>exceptional</kanji> dish you are cooking up.
kanji.meaningHint: Mix those pig mouths and foreheads in your pot. Think of how exceptional your soup looks.
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>Exceptional</kanji> pig mouth forehead soup is a favorite of Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち. You see him sniffing around your window, then he knocks and waves.
kanji.readingHint: "Can I have some of your exceptional soup?" Grandpa ごういち asks. "It smells so swell."

kanji.meaningMnemonic: See that <radical>gold</radical> man <radical>oversee</radical>ing everyone? He's a <kanji>model</kanji> human. His goldenness is a sign of how exemplary he is, and he's overseeing all of us flawed, regular folks to try to help us get on his level.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this golden man standing on a raised platform, looking down at you judgmentally with his arms crossed. You mutter curses at him under your breath, but really you're just jealous you're not a model human like him.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you look closer at the golden <kanji>model</kanji> human, you realize he's actually Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! No wonder he's covered in gold and overseeing everyone — he's the greatest conqueror in history!
kanji.readingHint: Hold on a sec — didn't Genghis Khan kill a bunch of people and father like a thousand kids? He doesn't seem like such a model person to you, but, hey, you don't make the rules around here — Genghis Khan does.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>building</radical>s are at a <radical>surplus</radical>. Because of that, we will need to <kanji>exclude</kanji> some of them from our future plans. Also, we'll need to <kanji>remove</kanji> some buildings too.
kanji.meaningHint: Say you're looking at a map of the city, with all the buildings laid out on it. There are too many, so you start marking which ones to exclude from the building plans. You also remove some buildings from the map all together.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Working with you on this project to <kanji>exclude</kanji> buildings from the plan, as well as <kanji>remove</kanji> buildings that already exist is little <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ).
kanji.readingHint: She's pointing out buildings to exclude / remove with great ferocity. "Remove this one. Exclude this one. Oh, and remove this one too."

kanji.meaningMnemonic: <radical>Wolverine</radical> is doing a <radical>construction</radical> job and using his <radical>mouth</radical> to take <radical>measurement</radical>s, so he knows how many folks he needs to <kanji>inquire</kanji> with about helping him. Using the mouth is a strange way to take measurements, but that's just how Wolverine does things. Once he measures it all out, he'll know how many construction workers he needs to inquire with about working for him.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine Wolverine going around, measuring the construction site with his mouth. Look at him with his mouth wide open, wandering around the site deep in thought. What a weirdo.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Another thing Wolverine has to <kanji>inquire</kanji> about is <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). He wants to make sure everyone on the construction site has good-fitting jeans to work in, so he inquires with them about their jean measurements.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine Wolverine handing you some new jeans at the construction site. Um, they're kind of all ripped up? Turns out that's just the style he likes. You'll be so fashionable now! Just like good old '90s Wolvie.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>poop</radical> twice on a sleeping <radical>drunkard</radical> <radical>person</radical> and you find yourself wondering <kanji>how many</kanji> more times you can poop on them and wonder <kanji>how much</kanji> they can take before they realize what’s going on.
kanji.meaningHint: You’ve already pooped twice on this drunkard, how many more times can you do it? How much do you think this person can take? Just imagine yourself doing this.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you continue thinking about <kanji>how much</kanji> poop this drunkard can take and <kanji>how many</kanji> more poops you can drop on him, he begins to wake up. Quickly, you <reading>eke</reading> (いく) out one last poop before you scurry away. You eke that poop out just in time to make your escape.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself as you eke out that last poop. Really concentrate on eking it out so you can get away in time. Flex your butt muscles for added effect.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>canopy</radical> <radical>guy</radical> is a guy who stands in the <kanji>corridor</kanji> trying to sell people canopies.
kanji.meaningHint: You walk through this corridor every day, and the canopy guy always accosts you with canopy sales. You wish there was another corridor you could take, but it's the only way to get where you want to go.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You take the <kanji>corridor</kanji>, but this time the canopy guy is gone. In his place is a new salesman. This one is trying to sell you <reading>roe</reading> (ろう).
kanji.readingHint: The roe salesman in the corridor is worse than the canopy salesman. They're both insistent, but the roe salesman smells bad.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Wrap your <radical>fingers</radical> around this <radical>broom</radical>, because it's time to <kanji>sweep</kanji>!
kanji.meaningHint: Keep your fingers tightly wrapped around the broom — if you drop it mid-sweep, all that dirt and dust will go flying everywhere and you'll have to start over from scratch.
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>Sweep</kanji>ing is good for the <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Every sweep of the broom makes your soul a little more pure. Cleanliness is next to godliness, after all.
kanji.readingHint: Feel your soul getting more and more purified as you sweep every inch of your house. Your soul's getting squeaky-clean, and it honestly feels amazing.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> came in and buried your <radical>flag</radical> so now you have to use a <radical>spoon</radical> and dig it out of the <kanji>mud</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine using a spoon to dig your flag outta all this mud. So much mud! Feel its cold, soiled wetness on your hands as you dig away.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You may know of Dora the Explorer. But have you heard about her dirty, <kanji>mud</kanji>dy brother, <reading>Doro</reading> (どろ)? They're like a team. And because of all the exploring he does, Doro is always covered in mud.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine them working together — Dora and Doro, the Muddy Explorers! Who knows why she's able to stay clean, while Doro gets so muddy…

rawData.reading_mnemonic: You may know of Dora the Explorer. But have you heard about her dirty, <kanji>mud</kanji>dy brother, <reading>Doro</reading> (どろ)? They're like a team. And because of all the exploring he does, Doro is always covered in mud.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> in <radical>spring</radical> that's nice and <radical>dry</radical> will make a good <kanji>pole</kanji> or <kanji>rod</kanji>. Break it out of the ground and give it a swing to see what a nice, sturdy <kanji>stick</kanji> it is!
kanji.meaningHint: Use this pole to hang your towels or curtains. Ooh, use it to pole vault!
kanji.readingMnemonic: After much deliberation, you decide to use this <kanji>pole</kanji> to go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). It's a pretty long pole, so you can just stand at the top of the lane and directly poke the bowling pins with it.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine all the irritated glares you get as you use your pole to cheat your way through several games of bowling. That's against the rules, buddy!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you can write an interesting <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> while riding on a <radical>horse</radical>, I’ll be pretty <kanji>surprised</kanji>. It’s hard enough to write a poem as it is, let alone an interesting one. And then you’re riding on a horse and the poem has to be about winter? Heck, I’d be surprised if you didn’t fall off the horse and injure yourself.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine trying to write a poem while riding a horse. It’d be really hard to do. I bet you’d be surprised if you managed to do it as well.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You might also be <kanji>surprised</kanji> to learn that you are to ride this horse all across <reading>きょう</reading>と. That gives you a time limit. You have until you traverse きょうと to write this poem about winter.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine riding through きょうと on a horse. As if that wasn’t difficult enough for poem writing, now you have all the sights and sounds of きょうと to distract you as well.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>mouth</radical> when you see a <radical>Chinese</radical> person and you <kanji>sigh</kanji>. You sigh so loud that it’s practically a roar coming out of your mouth. Chinese people and Chinese things make you sigh because it reminds you of when you stayed in China and it brings up memories. The sigh is a sigh of nostalgia.
kanji.meaningHint: Let out a long sigh as you recall memories from China and all the Chinese things you saw and did. Oh, sigh.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Your favorite Chinese thing to do is <reading>tan</reading> (たん). You can wander around without your shirt on and tan all day. No one will <kanji>sigh</kanji> or scoff at you, it’s perfectly acceptable. The Chinese just love to get their tan on.
kanji.readingHint: Sigh again as you remember how nice it was to tan everywhere. Picture yourself wandering around some Chinese streets with your shirt off as you soak in the rays and get a tan.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>warehouse</radical> radical and the <kanji>warehouse</kanji> kanji are the same! You can call it a <kanji>storehouse</kanji> too.
kanji.meaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You have a <kanji>warehouse</kanji> to store all of your <reading>sou</reading>ls (そう). When people or animals you know pass away, you take their souls and store them in your dry warehouse for safekeeping. It's a public service, really.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine if you kept a bunch of souls in a warehouse. This way they can't haunt anyone. And they can't get lost out in the spirit world. The warehouse is best for them.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>child</radical> shares his <radical>lineage</radical> with a <kanji>grandchild</kanji>. The child passes his lineage down to his child and then that child’s child is the grandchild and between them all a lineage is shared.
kanji.meaningHint: Look at this family tree. See the child, and then the grandchild. Trace the lineage with your finger as you observe.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The <reading>son</reading> (そん) of a son is a <kanji>grandchild</kanji>. In this case, a grandson. Easy.
kanji.readingHint: Just remember that a son is all you really want. You need a son to carry on the family name and the lineage. So make sure that your child and your grandchild are both sons.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Birds use <radical>grass</radical> and <radical>fruit</radical> to make their <kanji>nest</kanji>s.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a cute little nest with a family of birds in it. It looks like it was woven together with bits of grass and strawberries, so it smells delicious too.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The bottom of the <kanji>nest</kanji> also seems to be padded with someone's <reading>su</reading>it (す). Did... did this bird attack a human for this?!
kanji.readingHint: Picture the nest, made of grass, fruit, and the suit of the angry salaryman who's shouting below the tree, and missing the top half of his suit.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mountain</radical> coming out of the <radical>ground</radical> that you want to climb, but your <radical>forehead</radical> needs a <radical>towel</radical> for all your sweat. You don't have a towel, so you take off your <kanji>belt</kanji> and tie it around your forehead.
kanji.meaningHint: The belt is easy to tighten and it catches all the sweat before it can fall into your eyes. It isn't quite as absorbent as a towel though.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The <kanji>belt</kanji> isn't absorbent enough, so you wrap it in your <reading>tie</reading> (たい) and then tie the whole thing around your forehead again.
kanji.readingHint: It works! Your belt tied in a tie is the perfect invention!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>tombstone</radical> to measure its <kanji>diameter</kanji>. You’re doing this to each tombstone, and if you loiter around long enough, you’ll soon know the diameter of every tombstone.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine loitering around a tombstone. You're leaning on it. You're staring at nothing. Oh, and you're also measuring its diameter.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You need to know the <kanji>diameter</kanji> of each tombstone so you can make <reading>ca</reading>kes (けい) for each one. But the cakes can't be wider than the tombstones, or they'll flop off onto the ground.
kanji.readingHint: These carefully measured tombstone-diameter cakes are your special way of saying thanks to the ghosts for letting you loiter around their graves.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>request</radical> that <radical>winter</radical> <kanji>rescue</kanji> you from the warmth of the other seasons.
kanji.meaningHint: It's so hot. You hate it. You really hope winter will get your request and come early to rescue you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You get a note in the mail from winter! This is it, you're going to be rescued! You open the note and it says, "I have <kanji>rescue</kanji>d you with this <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう)." A cucumber falls out of the note.
kanji.readingHint: This cucumber is supposed to be cool and refreshing enough to rescue you from the heat. I guess it was too much to expect winter to come early just for you. At least cucumbers are yummy.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you play <radical>blackjack</radical> on the <radical>moon</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical>, your cards will <kanji>scatter</kanji> everywhere. The cards scatter because the gravity is so weak on the moon, so the blackjack game is prone to disaster.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine playing blackjack on the moon where even the slightest move causes the cards to scatter about. Watch them float along. It’s very frustrating to play this way.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You are playing blackjack with <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん). Santa-san is doing his best to use his Christmas magic to prevent the <kanji>scatter</kanji> of cards, but it’s not working out so well. Good effort, Santa-san.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself next to Santa-san as he tries to prevent the cards from scattering. Look at the expression of concentration on Santa-san’s face as he tries to prevent the scatter.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you grind <radical>rice</radical>, every <radical>part</radical> of it, eventually it will become a fine <kanji>powder</kanji>. You can then use this rice powder as a part to create some other dish. Some kind of cake, perhaps.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself grinding this rice into a powder. Hear the scrape of the rice on stone as you grind it down to the finest powder.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Actually, grinding the rice down to a <kanji>powder</kanji> is super <reading>fun</reading> (ふん). It’s so fun you don’t want to stop! AH HAHAHAHA! RICE POWDER! SO FUN!
kanji.readingHint: Okay, we got a little carried away there. Just keep in mind how fun this is. Make sure you say fun with a little bit of a Scottish accent. You know what they say, Scots have more fun!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>moon</radical> while you sit on a <radical>cliff</radical>, you inject a syringe of <radical>tofu</radical> right into your <kanji>vein</kanji>. Ahh, tofu. You're addicted to it. You sneak out to the cliff at night to get your tofu fix, injecting it straight into your vein.
kanji.meaningHint: Feel the tofu running through your veins. Feel the tofu goodness spreading all over your body. It's so intense you can even taste a little tofu in your mouth.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Eventually, your friends and family find you at the cliff, tripping on tofu. Each and every <kanji>vein</kanji> on your body is engorged, pulsating with the effects of tofu. Your friends try and take you home. "You cyan't <reading>myake</reading> (みゃく) me!" you slur, suffering from the effects of the tofu. "None of you cyan myake me go home! You cyan’t myake me! Nyah!"
kanji.readingHint: Say this out loud, it'll help you remember. Really focus on the "myake" be sure to say it like you're all bugged out from tofu overdose.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You want some <radical>flowers</radical> so you take your <radical>cleat</radical> to the side of a <radical>tree</radical> hoping they'll fall down, but you get a <kanji>vegetable</kanji> instead. What? You kick the tree again and another, different vegetable falls out.
kanji.meaningHint: Now you have two vegetables and no flowers. Do vegetables even grow on trees?
kanji.readingMnemonic: You feed these <kanji>vegetable</kanji>s (that you didn't even want) to your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). It only needs a little though, since its stomach is so thin, so you just make it hold the rest.
kanji.readingHint: Picture your cyborg nibbling on the vegetables you cleat-kicked out of this tree. You give all of the things you don't like to your cyborg nowadays. Which is probably why there are so many weird things all over it...

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You want to <radical>change</radical> your <radical>shellfish</radical> into <kanji>freight</kanji> so you can transport it. That means packing them up into giant freight containers.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine changing your shellfish from loose, wet form, to solid freight form. It's actually a lot of hard work.
kanji.readingMnemonic: To move this <kanji>freight</kanji>, you need a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You strap all of the freight containers to the top of your car, stacked nice and high and precarious, so you can move it.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> of (odd-looking) <radical>lion</radical>s puts a <radical>drop</radical> of magic liquid on his face to look like a <radical>person</radical> who <kanji>resemble</kanji>s someone else. The leader of lions is going incognito, so he needs to resemble a person who is not himself. He takes this drop of powerful face paint and transforms.
kanji.meaningHint: Watch this leader as the transformation takes place. Now he doesn’t resemble anything like the leader he used to be. He doesn’t look like the same person. Watch him morph in your head.
kanji.readingMnemonic:
Now he <kanji>resemble</kanji>s an even greater leader, and all the lions get down on one <reading>knee</reading> (に) to pay their respects.

(Note that we recently changed this kanji's main reading from ね to に, because ね is an irregular reading.)

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You have some <radical>dirt</radical> next to a <radical>pool</radical> that has an extra <radical>drop</radical> of water in it because you need them to be <kanji>equal</kanji>. If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine seeing a pool that isn't equal. It's like a tiny kiddy pool with an inch of water. And it's surrounded by mounds of dirt. No no no, this won't do—add more water to make them equal.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You spend a few hours making sure the water-to-dirt ratio is perfectly <kanji>equal</kanji>, not for yourself, but because the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England told you to. You may agree that equality is important, but it's the King's responsibility to keep things that way. Which is why he ordered you, small peon, to do the equalizing work for him.
kanji.readingHint: Okay, so the existence of a king kind of makes things not, well, equal, for all people. But at least aesthetically everything will be!

If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.
rawData.reading_mnemonic: You spend a few hours making sure the water-to-dirt ratio is perfectly <kanji>equal</kanji>, not for yourself, but because the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England told you to. You may agree that equality is important, but it's the King's responsibility to keep things that way. Which is why he ordered you, small peon, to do the equalizing work for him.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Inside your <radical>greenhouse</radical> there's a mound of <radical>dirt</radical> which appears to be someone's <kanji>grave</kanji>. When you dig into it, you discover that it's an entire <kanji>tomb</kanji>, with doors and passages and everything!
kanji.meaningHint: Why would someone put a grave in a greenhouse? Is this one of those "green burials" everyone's talking about these days? Having the whole tomb complex seems a tad counterproductive, though…
kanji.readingMnemonic: You pry open the <kanji>grave</kanji> inside the inner chamber of the <kanji>tomb</kanji>, and are shocked to find the mummified body of <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the Clown staring back at you! How terrifying!
kanji.readingHint: You watch in horror as Bobo raises his arms and slowly rises from his grave. "At last, I have awoken from my slumber… Prepare yourselves, mortals — Bobo's taking reservations for birthday parties again!"

kanji.meaningMnemonic: On your <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>scarecrow</radical> because you're <kanji>rich</kanji>. You want to scare away all the poor, non-rich people so you put a scarecrow on your roof.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine looking up at the roof at this scarecrow. There's a sign around its neck that says, "Rich people welcome." Ah, you're safe. Time to go to bed!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You wake up the next morning and all of your <kanji>rich</kanji>es have been stolen! You call the police and they call you a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ)! Pardon me?! "You basically put an advertisement on your roof telling everyone you're rich. Of course you got robbed, you fool."
kanji.readingHint: You poor sad, no longer rich fool.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You're <radical>loiter</radical>ing, staring down at the <radical>cross</radical> that got caught in the <radical>net</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical>, because it's trying to teach you to act with <kanji>virtue</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: People who keep the cross in their hearts are supposed to be full of virtue, always doing the right thing, always helping others. But you aren't sure if you want to be virtuous, it's a lot of responsibility. So you look down at this symbolic cross and loiter instead of making a decision.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you try to decide if you want to live a life of <kanji>virtue</kanji> the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you. He points to your heart and the cross, then points to his own and you see a glowing cross in his own.
kanji.readingHint: I don't know about you, but I'm pretty sure Tokugawa Ieyasu is telling you to choose a life of virtue.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> to your <radical>forehead</radical> and stand your <radical>legs</radical> high up in a <radical>tree</radical> if you want to <kanji>look for</kanji> something. After all, shielding your eyes and getting up high is the best way to look for something, isn’t it?
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine it's like a quest from a book or game. What are you looking for? Maybe a powerful sword, or a lost princess. Picture it in your mind, this thing you're looking for.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You <kanji>look for</kanji> it for so long that you can't help getting a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). I mean, you're up some tree, shielding your eyes from the sun as you look for that thing, and it's been hours, days? Of course you're going to get a tan.
kanji.readingHint: Feel the sun's rays on your skin as you search and search and search. Watch as your skin gets darker, crispier, and — hmm — starts smelling like cooked meat. Now that's a good tan.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> sticks his <radical>toe</radical> into a <radical>shellfish</radical>. You ask him why and he says he wants to <kanji>spy</kanji> on it.
kanji.meaningHint: There’s a hidden camera on his toe and the leader wants to have plenty of secret spy footage to review later.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You and the leader review the <kanji>spy</kanji> footage and find a single <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tot hidden within the shellfish. Together you uncover a huge smuggling ring. Clams, oysters, mussels, you name it! Shellfish of all kinds are using their bodies to sneak tater tots to the undersea world.
kanji.readingHint: If it wasn't for your toe spy network, these egregious tater tot crimes would have never reached the light of day.

vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This word uses the kun'yomi readings. Since you didn't learn that reading for 粉 with the kanji, here's a mnemonic to help you out. What sort of people like <vocabulary>wheat flour</vocabulary> the most? <reading>子</reading>! 子 like wheat flour the most since it is both fun to eat and to play with. Silly 子, wheat flour is a food, not a toy.

rawData.meaning_mnemonic: <kanji>Wheat</kanji> <kanji>powder</kanji> is <vocabulary>wheat flour</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: This is something made by kneading wheat flour.
context_sentences.ja: これは麦粉をこねて作ったものです。

context_sentences.en: At home, we only had a very small amount of wheat flour left.
context_sentences.ja: 家にはもう、ほんの少しの麦粉しか残っていませんでした。

context_sentences.en: In Mesopotamia, they used to knead wheat flour with water to make flatbread to eat.
context_sentences.ja: メソポタミアでは、麦粉を水でねって平焼きパンにして食べていたそうです。

context_sentences.en: Today I opened a bag of wheat flour for the first time in long time and found a bunch of green worms wriggling in it.
context_sentences.ja: 今日久々に麦粉が入った袋を開けたら、緑色のクネクネした幼虫が大量発生していました。
